title: Winning Planning Interviews
paras: Winning planning interviews. From graduate to director — how planning interviews really work, and how to win them. This is a United Kingdom guide, for 2026.

Let me start with something that will save you a great deal of anxiety. In more than thirty years recruiting planners, from graduates to directors, I've found the interview rarely decides who gets the job. By the time you sit down, the employer has usually already formed a view — from your application, your C-V, your LinkedIn, your recommendations, the recruiter's feedback, and often a reputation that preceded you. The interview is mostly there to confirm or contradict an impression that already exists. Candidates who understand that relax into the right task. Not performing a role — but confirming, calmly and specifically, that the person on paper is real.

The other thing worth knowing is that planning interviews are not generic. Most interview advice is written by people who've never sat on a planning panel — and it shows. A local-authority interview for a development-management officer is a different animal from a housebuilder's land-team interview, which is different again from a director-level appointment at a consultancy. The questions, the panel, the unspoken tests, and the definition of a good answer all shift. And crucially, they shift as you rise.

title: Part 1 — How planning recruitment really works
paras: Part one — how planning recruitment really works. Understanding who is in the room, and who decides, changes how you prepare. Recruitment is rarely one person's call. In a local authority, a panel typically includes the hiring manager — a team leader or development-management manager — often a more senior planner or head of service, sometimes someone from H-R, and, for senior posts, an elected member. H-R usually runs the process and guards fairness. They rarely decide, but they can veto. The hiring manager's view carries the most weight, because they have to work with you. In the private sector it's more commercial, and often less formal. An associate or director who needs a fee-earner. Sometimes a two-stage process — a technical conversation, then a cultural one — with the decision resting with whoever owns the team's profit and loss.

Two things follow from that. First, fit matters more than candidates like to admit. Not as a euphemism for sameness, but the panel honestly asking — do we want to work with this person, and can we trust them? Second, technical staff have real influence. The senior planner who quizzes you on policy may not be the decision-maker, but a doubtful word from them afterwards can sink you — and an enthusiastic one can carry you. So treat everyone in the process, including the recruiter and the person who books the room, as someone whose opinion counts. It usually does.

title: Part 2 — Do your homework
paras: Part two — do your homework. Preparation is where interviews are won, and it's what average candidates most neglect. Research four things — the employer, the panel, the job and the place — and you'll walk in knowing more about the role than half the panel.

Start with the employer, and go well beyond the website. Recent schemes and appeal decisions — the Inspectorate names the players, so you can read their actual casework. Their clients and sectors. Their standing in the trade press. Company news, and growth plans. Then the panel. Research every interviewer on LinkedIn — background, tenure, R-T-P-I involvement, anything they've written — then use it lightly, to build rapport. Never parade it, and never stray into their personal life. And then decode the job itself. Take the job description apart sentence by sentence. Separate the essential from the desirable. Read the hidden requirements — 'manage a varied caseload' means resilience and organisation; 'work with members and the public' means political awareness and calm. And prepare one concrete example for every requirement.

One resource candidates badly underuse is the recruiter. A good specialist has often placed people with this employer before, sat through their feedback, and knows the panel, the format, and what sank the last candidate. Ask them directly what the employer is really looking for. Their fee depends on your success — so treat them as a coach on your side, not a gate to get past.

title: Part 3 — The interview changes as you rise
paras: Part three — the interview changes as you rise. This is the heart of the guide. The single biggest mistake experienced planners make is interviewing the way they did ten years ago — proving they're technically competent, when the panel stopped doubting that long ago, and is asking something else entirely. A director candidate who spends the interview proving they understand the N-P-P-F has misread the room. As you rise, the weight shifts — from technical mastery, to leadership, commercial value and trust.

I've watched capable senior planners talk themselves out of director jobs by answering like the brilliant senior planner they used to be — all technical mastery, no commercial or people dimension — while a less dazzling rival, who talked about winning work, growing staff and carrying risk, walked away with the offer. The panel wasn't choosing the better planner. It was choosing the person who understood which job they were interviewing for. Work out which conversation you're in — and have that one.

title: Part 4 — The planning question bank
paras: Part four — the planning question bank. The written guide gives you a working bank of real planning interview questions, level by level, with what each one is really testing — because the words on the surface, and the thing being assessed, are rarely the same. Prepare a genuine example for each question that applies to you.
The bank is illustrative — representative of real UK planning interviews across levels, in 2025 and 2026.

title: Part 5 — Technical questions
paras: Part five — technical questions. Technical questions test knowledge — but more than that, they test whether you can apply it, and whether you'll bluff when you shouldn't. The gravest error is pretending to know something you don't. A confident wrong answer worries a panel far more than an honest 'I haven't dealt with that directly, but my understanding is this — and I'd check X'. Never bluff.
The depth expected rises with seniority — at director level, these questions are rarely the focus. And confirm the current legal position before you rely on any technical detail.

title: Part 6 — Answering well: STAR, and standing out
paras: Part six — answering well. STAR, and standing out. For any 'tell me about a time' question, answer with STAR. Set out the Situation, briefly. The Task you faced. The Action you took — that's the heart of it, and it's always what you did, not what 'we' did. And the Result — with a number, where you honestly can. Use STAR for competency and scenario questions. Not for simple factual or opinion questions, where a crisp, direct answer is better. And the trick to not sounding rehearsed is to know your stories, not your scripts. Have six or seven real examples ready, and tell them naturally, adapting to the question — rather than memorising word-for-word answers that collapse the moment the question is phrased differently.

Let me show you the difference. Take the question — a member goes against your recommendation. The average answer: 'I'd be professional about it and accept the committee's decision.' True — but empty.

The excellent answer sounds like this. 'It happened on a housing scheme in a sensitive village. I'd given a clear, evidenced recommendation; members refused on character grounds. I made sure the reasons were sound and defensible, supported the decision publicly as the committee's to make, and briefed the applicant honestly on appeal prospects. We defended it at appeal, and it was dismissed — the members' judgement held. I don't take these personally. Committees exist to weigh things differently, and my job is advice good enough to stand up either way.'
Specific, calm, politically literate — it shows judgement, rather than claiming it.

You demonstrate commercial awareness by talking about value, fees, risk and delivery. Professional judgement, by showing how you weigh things. Integrity, by owning a mistake honestly. Calmness, by how you discuss a crisis. And leadership, by talking about the people you developed. Anyone can say 'I have excellent judgement'. Only a good candidate can prove it — with a case where their judgement was tested, and held.

title: Part 7 — Presentations and virtual interviews
paras: Part seven — presentations, and virtual interviews. First, presentations. Many senior, and some mid-level, interviews now include a presentation — typically ten to fifteen minutes on a set brief. How would you grow this team. Your approach to a live site. A market you would target. It tests how you think, structure and communicate under a little pressure — exactly what the job demands in front of clients and committees. So structure it simply. A clear opening that states your answer. Three or four substantiated points. And a short close that returns to the brief. Keep the slides few and clean — six to ten, minimal text, one idea per slide. Rehearse to time, because overrunning reads as poor judgement. Say the thing, prove it, stop.

Now, virtual interviews. Interviews on Teams, Zoom or Meet are routine, and a poor setup undercuts a strong candidate. So — a plain, tidy background. The camera at eye level. Light on your face from the front, not a window behind you. A decent microphone, and a tested connection. And your eyes to the camera, not to your own image. Dress as you would in person. A few discreet notes off-camera are fine — that's one quiet advantage of remote — but don't read from them. And if the technology fails, stay calm, suggest switching to the phone, and carry on. How you handle a glitch is itself a small test of composure.

title: Part 8 — The questions you should ask
paras: Part eight — the questions you should ask. When they ask, 'do you have any questions for us?', the wrong answer is no. It reads as disinterest, and it wastes your best chance to leave a strong final impression. So here are questions that work well.

First — what does success look like in this role in the first year? That shows you think in outcomes, and it tells you how you'll be judged. Then — what are the biggest challenges facing the team right now? That signals realism, and invites an honest, revealing answer. How is the team structured, and how does work come in? That's commercial curiosity — especially strong in the private sector. What does progression look like from here? Ambition, and thinking beyond the job itself. How would you describe the culture here — honestly? That shows fit matters to you too, and it often gets a candid reply. For a local authority — what are the service's main priorities and pressures? That shows you understand the real context. And finally — is there anything about my experience you'd like me to expand on? That one is confident. A last chance to close any doubt.
Two cautions. Don't make your only questions about pay, leave and hours — there is a time for those, and it isn't now. And never ask something a moment on their website would have answered.

title: Part 9 — What employers are really deciding
paras: Part nine — what employers are really deciding. Beneath every planning interview, whatever the questions, the panel is deciding a small number of very human things. Confidence against arrogance — quiet certainty impresses, bluster alarms. Your professional judgement. Your commercial thinking. Your self-awareness, and resilience. Your integrity. And your emotional intelligence. Technical ability gets you shortlisted; these qualities get you hired. The most useful way to understand it is this — the panel is silently asking a series of trust questions.

Would we trust you with a difficult client — alone? Would we let you go to committee and speak for us? Would we be comfortable leaving you in front of an important developer, or a room of angry residents? Would we hand you the contentious case — and sleep at night? Would we want to promote you — and would others follow you?
Everything you say either builds or erodes the answer. A candidate who grasps this stops trying to impress, and starts trying to reassure — which, paradoxically, is far more impressive.

title: Part 10 — Negotiating the offer
paras: Part ten — negotiating the offer. Negotiate after the offer, when your leverage is highest and they've decided they want you — not before. Know your whole package, and know the market rate — PlanRef's Salaries and Progression guide covers this in detail. And be ready to discuss more than base pay. Bonus. Car allowance. Pension. Annual leave. Hybrid working. Professional subscriptions. Training and C-P-D. Promotion prospects. Probation, and notice. Ask professionally, and from evidence — not need. In a candidate's market, a reasonable request rarely offends. And treat counter-offers from your current employer with real caution. They solve the money, but seldom the reasons you looked — and many who accept them have moved on within the year. A good recruiter will negotiate for you at arm's length.

title: Part 11 — Interview mistakes, ranked
paras: Part eleven — interview mistakes, ranked. Roughly from the most serious — the ones that end a candidacy on the spot — down to the smaller habits that quietly cost people offers. Most candidates make only one or two, usually without realising.

First, the serious ones — these end interviews. Bluffing a technical answer — a confident wrong answer worries a panel more than an honest 'I don't know'. Lying or exaggerating — and being caught, which collapses everything else you said. Badmouthing a current or former employer — it says more about you than about them. Breaching confidentiality — discussing live cases or clients in detail. That one is instantly disqualifying. Arrogance — dismissing others, talking over the panel, treating the interview as beneath you. Criticising a scheme the interviewer worked on — do your homework, so you never blunder into it. Having done no research — obvious within minutes, and unforgivable. Being unable to talk through a single real case — for an experienced planner, fatal. Blaming others for every setback — no accountability, no trust. And rudeness to junior staff or the recruiter — it always gets back to the panel.

Then the costly ones — these lose offers. Rambling, unstructured answers that never reach a point. Vague, 'we'-heavy answers that hide what you actually did. Not knowing the authority's housing land supply, or its Local Plan position. At senior level, still trying to prove technical competence instead of leadership. Reciting policy instead of applying it. Memorised, robotic answers that collapse when the question is rephrased. No questions for the panel — or only questions about pay and leave. Talking too much, and never letting the panel steer. Talking too little — one-line answers that give them nothing to hire. Overrunning a presentation, or reading the slides aloud. A poor virtual setup — dark, looming, glitchy, distracted. Failing to give concrete examples — all claim, no evidence. Negotiating hard before an offer is even on the table. Showing no genuine interest in this employer specifically. And being unable to explain your reasons for leaving convincingly.

And finally, the avoidable ones — these leave a dent. Arriving flustered, or logging on late. Dressing too casually, and misreading the employer's formality. Fidgeting, poor eye contact, low energy. Not preparing questions, and asking something the website answered. Failing to link your answers back to their role and their needs. Undue modesty — never quite saying what you achieved. Waffling when you don't know, instead of giving a clean, honest answer. Forgetting the names or roles of the people interviewing you. No follow-up — missing the short, courteous thank-you that costs nothing. And treating a so-called junior interviewer as unimportant.

title: Part 12 — The four-week preparation plan
paras: Part twelve — the four-week preparation plan. Preparation spread over a month beats a panic the night before. And if you have shorter notice, compress the plan proportionately.

title: The ten habits of highly employable planners
paras: And now, the ten habits of highly employable planners. Interviews are won in the years before them, not the hour of them. These are the long-term habits that separate the planners who are always in demand from the ones who struggle.

One — they keep learning. C-P-D is a habit, not a box; a stale planner is soon a stuck one. Two — they become known for something. A specialism. 'The one for energy consents.' 'The one who wins the hard appeals.' Three — they tell the truth. To clients, members, colleagues and interviewers. Integrity is the whole foundation. Four — they deliver. Reliably, and they finish what they start. Nothing builds a reputation faster, or quieter. Five — they think commercially. Even in the public sector — value, risk and delivery, not only policy. Six — they stay calm. Under pressure, in hostile rooms, when it goes wrong. Composure is remembered. Seven — they help others. They mentor, share, and lift juniors — and they're recommended warmly for it, for years. Eight — they keep their reputation in order. C-V current, LinkedIn strong, footprint clean — ready to be found. Nine — they build relationships before they need them. A real network, tended in the good times. And ten — they own their mistakes. Admit, learn, move on — which, far from damaging trust, is what earns it.

Do these, steadily, and the interview looks after itself. You'll walk in as someone the panel already half-wants to hire. You'll spend an hour confirming what your reputation promised. And you'll spend far less of your career chasing jobs — because the good ones will be coming to find you.

One closing note. This guide was compiled in 2026 for PlanRef, and draws on long UK planning-recruitment experience. The recruitment statistics cited elsewhere in the Careers section are attributed to their sources — StandOut C-V, the C-I-P-D, and the National Careers Service. Interview practice evolves — so treat the craft here as informed guidance, and the technical detail as a prompt to confirm the current position before you rely on it.
